title: How interoceptive awareness rebuilds the brain-body connection
author: Kalesh
excerpt: I used to think healing was all in my head. I spent years in therapy talking about my childhood, analyzing my patterns, trying to think my way out of a body th...
metaDescription: I used to think healing was all in my head. I spent years in therapy talking about my childhood, analyzing my patterns, trying to think my way out of a bo...
htmlContent:
I used to think healing was all in my head. I spent years in therapy talking about my childhood, analyzing my patterns, trying to think my way out of a body that felt like a war zone. And it helped, don't get me wrong. But something was missing. I could name every trauma response in the book, but I still couldn't feel my feet on the ground when panic hit. I still couldn't tell the difference between being tired and being dissociated. I still couldn't feel hunger until I was starving, or thirst until my lips were cracked.

Here's the thing. Your brain is not the boss of you. Not really. Your brain is part of a system, and that system runs through your entire body. The vagus nerve, that long wandering highway of communication, connects your brain to your heart, your lungs, your gut. And the conversation it carries is not just one way. Your body talks back. It whispers. It shouts. It sends signals constantly. The question is whether you're listening.

What I'm talking about is interoceptive awareness. It's a mouthful, I know. But it's simple at its core. Interoception is your ability to sense what's happening inside your body. The beating of your heart. The expansion of your lungs. The gurgle of your stomach. The ache in your shoulders. The flutter of anxiety. The warmth of safety. This is not some woo-woo concept. This is hard science. And it might be the most underrated skill in trauma recovery.

The Body's Intelligence System

Think about what your body does every second without your conscious input. Your heart beats. Your lungs breathe. Your pupils dilate. Your digestion churns. Your immune system fights. Your endocrine system releases hormones in precise rhythms. You don't direct any of this. You're not sitting there thinking, "Okay, now release some cortisol. Now constrict those blood vessels." It all happens automatically.

But here's where it gets interesting. That automatic system is not completely separate from your conscious awareness. You can learn to sense it. You can learn to read its signals. And when you do, you gain something : choice.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, has spent decades showing how trauma lives in the body. He talks about how animals in the wild shake off their stress responses after a threat passes. They don't carry it forward. But humans do. We freeze. We collapse. We hold the tension in our tissues. And then we wonder why we feel stuck.

Interoceptive awareness is the skill that lets you notice when you're holding that tension. It's the difference between being in a state and being able to observe that state. It's the difference between being the storm and watching the clouds pass.

What Happens When You Can't Feel Your Body

For many of us with trauma histories, interoception is impaired. It's not that we don't have internal signals. It's that we've learned to ignore them. Or we never learned to recognize them in the first place.

When you grow up in an unpredictable environment, your nervous system learns that safety is not guaranteed. Your body learns to stay on high alert. And that constant state of activation can drown out the quieter signals. Hunger becomes a vague irritability. Exhaustion becomes a fog you can't name. Sadness becomes a headache that won't go away.

Stay with me here. This is not a failure on your part. This is adaptation. Your body did exactly what it needed to do to survive. But the same adaptations that kept you alive in a dangerous environment can keep you stuck in a safe one.

I remember the first time a therapist asked me to notice what I was feeling in my body. I stared at her blankly. "I don't know," I said. "Nothing?" That was a lie. There was plenty happening. My shoulders were up near my ears. My jaw was clenched. My stomach was in knots. But I had no practice sensing any of it. I lived in my head, a floating consciousness disconnected from the flesh that carried it around.

This is where the research of Stephen Porges becomes essential. His Polyvagal Theory explains how our nervous system has three main states: ventral vagal (safety and connection), sympathetic (fight or flight), and dorsal vagal (freeze or collapse). And here's the key: you can't consciously choose which state you're in. But you can learn to notice which state you're in. And that noticing is the first step toward shifting.

When you can feel your heart racing, you know you're in sympathetic activation. When you feel your limbs heavy and your mind foggy, you know you're in dorsal collapse. When you feel your chest open and your breath easy, you know you're in ventral safety. Without interoception, you're just along for the ride. With it, you have a map.

How to Build Interoceptive Awareness

I'm not going to tell you to sit in lotus position and chant. That's not how this works for most of us. Building interoceptive awareness is a gradual, gentle process. It's about small moments of attention, not forced meditation marathons.

Start with one signal. Just one. Maybe it's your breath. Maybe it's the feeling of your feet on the floor. Maybe it's the weight of your body in your chair. Notice it for five seconds. That's it. Five seconds. Then go back to whatever you were doing. Do this ten times a day. It feels ridiculous at first. It feels like nothing. But you're building a pathway. You're teaching your brain that these signals matter.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium glycinate is known for its ability to support relaxation without the digestive upset that other forms can cause. For me, it helped quiet the background noise in my body just enough that I could start to notice what was actually there. It's not a cure. But it can make the first steps a little easier.

Another approach is to pair interoceptive practice with movement. You don't need a full yoga practice. Just walk slowly and notice how your feet feel against the ground. Stretch your arms overhead and notice the sensation in your ribs. Shake out your hands and notice the tingling. The body speaks in sensation. You're just learning its language.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She breaks down Polyvagal Theory into actionable steps. No jargon. No shame. Just a clear path toward understanding your own nervous system. I found it helpful because it gave me a framework for what I was experiencing. It made the chaos feel less random.

The Resistance You Will Feel

Let me be honest with you. Building interoceptive awareness is not always pleasant. When you start paying attention to your body, you might notice things you've been avoiding. The chronic tension. The old pain. The grief that lives in your chest. The anger that tightens your jaw. The fear that sits in your stomach like a stone.

This is normal. This is part of the process. Your body has been holding these things for years, decades even. It's not going to release them all at once. And it's not going to release them just because you noticed them. But noticing is the first step. You can't release what you can't feel.

Wild, right? The very thing we've been avoiding is the thing we need to turn toward. But we don't turn toward it with force. We turn toward it with curiosity. With gentleness. With the understanding that these sensations are not threats. They are information.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," has shown how trauma literally reshapes the brain and body. He talks about how talk therapy alone is often not enough because the trauma is stored in places words can't reach. The body needs to be involved in the healing. Interoceptive awareness is one of the most direct ways to bring the body into the room.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link) by Oprah Winfrey and Bruce Perry. It shifts the question from "What's wrong with you?" to "What happened to you?" That small shift changes everything. It takes the shame out of the equation. It allows you to approach your own body with understanding instead of judgment.

And a book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link) by Gabor Mate. He shows how suppressed emotion and chronic stress can as physical disease. His work is a stark reminder that ignoring the body's signals comes at a cost. Interoceptive awareness is not just about feeling better emotionally. It's about protecting your physical health.

The Rewiring That Happens

When you practice interoceptive awareness consistently, something shifts. It's not dramatic. It's not a lightning bolt. It's gradual. It's the slow rewiring of neural pathways. It's the strengthening of the insula, the part of the brain that processes internal body signals. It's the quieting of the amygdala, the alarm system that has been screaming for years.

You start to notice things earlier. You catch the rising tide of anxiety before it becomes a wave. You notice the dip in energy before you crash. You recognize the signs of dissociation before you disappear completely. And with that early recognition comes the possibility of intervention. You can breathe. You can move. You can reach out. You can do something other than react.

This is not about control. Let me be clear. You will never have complete control over your nervous system. That's not how it works. But you can have influence. You can have choice. You can have the ability to shift, even slightly, even temporarily. And those small shifts add up over time.

I still have days where I can't feel my body. Days where I'm disconnected, floating, lost in my head. But those days are shorter now. They're less frequent. And when they happen, I know what's going on. I can name it. I can say to myself, "Okay, I'm dissociating. This is a dorsal vagal response. I need to do something to ground." And sometimes I can do it. And sometimes I can't. But even the knowing matters.

Think about it. Your body has been talking to you your whole life. It's been sending signals, sending messages, sending distress calls. And you've been trained to ignore them. Trained by trauma. Trained by culture. Trained by a world that values productivity over presence. But you can learn a new way. You can learn to listen.

It takes time. It takes patience. It takes a willingness to feel uncomfortable things. But the reward is a life lived in your body instead of above it. A life where you can feel joy in your chest instead of just thinking about it. A life where you can cry fully instead of holding it in. A life where you can rest without guilt because you can actually feel how tired you are.

That's the rewiring. That's the healing. It's not about becoming someone new. It's about coming home to the body you've always had.
